coredata.dc:title: Using the photo-enhanced barrier effect on electrochemical response for highly sensitive detection of melamine
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yichang
Liao, Yiquan
Zhang, Bin
Xu, Ruiqi
Ma, Ye
Zhao, Minggang
Cui, Hongzhi
coredata.dc:description: Melamine added to milk powder can lead to kidney injury and even death, but rapid detection is still hard due to the strong interference of milk powder solution. Herein, the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh was constructed to detect melamine by using the photo-enhanced barrier effects on electrochemical response. Schottky barrier was regulated effectively to produce electrochemical response to melamine by photo-induced electrostatic interaction, which exhibited strong resistance to interference in milk powder solution. Sensitivity was enhanced by nearly 5 times and the lowest detection limit was reduced as low as 0.274 nM. The obtained high recovery (100%-104%) and good stability in milk powder solution indicated the good potential for practical applications. It provides a new opportunity for achieving strong resistance to interference by using the photo-enhanced barrier driving effect on electrochemical response.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heterojunction
Carbon nano-point
Energy barrier
Sensor
Melamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the prepared CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was sponsored by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52072353, 22106152); Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (202042009, 202113030); Fellowship of China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M672146); Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2021QB059).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM images of the CC mesh. (b) High-magnification SEM image of the CC/CeO2 mesh. (c) TEM image, (d) SEM images and (e) High-magnification SEM image of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh. (f) HRTEM image of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh.

CV curves of the prepared CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh in presence and absence of 0.5 mg/mL (a) positively charged, (b) uncharged and (c) negatively charged Hb in PBS, respectively. The corresponding changes of the Schottky barrier after the addition of (d) positively charged, (e) uncharged and (f) negatively charged Hb.
(a) Adsorption of melamine by the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh. (b) The corresponding energy band diagram of the CeO2/CNPs Schottky junction for adsorption of melamine.

DPV responses of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh to 40 nM positively charged melamine (a) in the dark, (b) under a 200 W Xe lamp and (c) under a 400 W Xe lamp. (d) Nyquist plot of the impedance of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh in the dark and under illumination with the addition of positively charged melamine. (e) Schottky barrier height decreases under illumination due to the increased photogenerated electrons in the conduction band. (f) Schottky barrier height increases with the adsorption of positively charged melamine under illumination.

DPV responses of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh to 0–100 nM positively charged melamine (a) in the dark, (b) under a 200 W Xe lamp and (c) under a 400 W Xe lamp. (d) The corresponding calibration curves under different illumination conditions.
Comparison of the sensing performance of various sensors to melamine.

head.ce:title: Using the photo-enhanced barrier effect on electrochemical response for highly sensitive detection of melamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, 266100 Qingdao, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, 266100 Qingdao, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Carbon cloth/CeO2/Carbon nano-points mesh was successfully used to detect melamine in milk powder by using the photo-enhanced barrier driving effect on electrochemical response. The Schottky barrier based sensing mechanism exhibited strong resistance to interference in milk, which made the sensitivity increase by nearly 5 times and the lowest detection limit reduce to 0.274 nM.

Melamine added to milk powder can lead to kidney injury and even death, but rapid detection is still hard due to the strong interference of milk powder solution. Herein, the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh was constructed to detect melamine by using the photo-enhanced barrier effects on electrochemical response. Schottky barrier was regulated effectively to produce electrochemical response to melamine by photo-induced electrostatic interaction, which exhibited strong resistance to interference in milk powder solution. Sensitivity was enhanced by nearly 5 times and the lowest detection limit was reduced as low as 0.274 nM. The obtained high recovery (100%-104%) and good stability in milk powder solution indicated the good potential for practical applications. It provides a new opportunity for achieving strong resistance to interference by using the photo-enhanced barrier driving effect on electrochemical response.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Melamine has a high nitrogen content and can be added to milk powder instead of protein to avoid detection. Excessive intake can cause kidney failure and urolithiasis (Q. Li, Song, & Wen, 2019), so melamine detection is of great importance for human health. However, the rapid detection of melamine is difficult due to the strong matrix interference of milk powder solution. Current detection methods for melamine, including capillary electrophoresis (Guo et al., 2020), fluorescence sensing (Z. Li, Li, Li, & Wang, 2019), high performance liquid chromatography (Tasci, Cubuk, Yetimoglu, & Kahraman, 2022), gas chromatography mass spectrometry (Osorio, Aznar, Nerin, Birse, Elliott, & Chevallier, 2020), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Shellaiah & Sun, 2019) and so on, require expensive instruments and complex protocols. Electrochemical detection has the advantages of high sensitivity, rapid response and low cost. However, melamine as a non-electroactive substance is difficult to be detected by traditional electrochemical detection based on REDOX reaction (Daizy, Tarafder, Al-Mamun, Liu, Aly Saad Aly, & Khan, 2019) (Dong, Zhu, Jia, Li, Ma, & Zhao, 2021). A facile and fast method for detecting melamine is thus desired to be developed.

Semiconductor heterojunctions have attracted much attention due to the unique photoelectric characteristics. Among them, Schottky junction can be well-tunable by temperature, magnetic field and light to obtain unique performance (Ma, Li, Li, & Zhao, 2022; Ma, Zhu, He, Zhao, & Cui, 2022; Nam et al., 2013). For example, the height of Schottky barrier at the Au/diamond interface was decreased by UV irradiation, which caused the current increase by 5 orders of magnitude (Lin et al., 2022). The results demonstrate light can adjust electron transport by regulating Schottky barrier. Since Schottky barrier has an obstructing effect on electron transport, it will also affect electrochemical current response in electrochemical detection. Therefore, it is interesting to study the light regulatory effect of Schottky barrier on electrochemical response.

It is well known that electrostatic induction can change the conduction band position of semiconductor by inducing charge, resulting in barrier height of heterojunction changing. As charged molecule can produce electrostatic induction, it can be used to regulate barrier height. Melamine with triamine groups (Viehrig et al., 2020) is positively charged at pH < 5.0 and negatively charged at pH > 9.0 in solution, so it can be used to regulate the height of Schottky barrier by electrostatic interaction. If the synergistic effect of light is further introduced, it is expected to produce a highly sensitive electrochemical response by achieving a high barrier regulation.

CeO2 is an n-type semiconductor with wide bandgap (2.72 eV) and high catalytic activity (Charbgoo, Ramezani, & Darroudi, 2017). Carbon nano-points (CNPs) have high electron transport efficiency (Molaei, 2020). The well-matched work function makes them form Schottky junctions (X. Li, Ma, Qian, Zhang, Zuo, & Yao, 2019; Nakajima & Mori, 2005; Wang, et al., 2022). Carbon cloth (CC) is a three-dimensional multilayer structure composed of carbon fibers (Fallatah, Almomtan, & Padalkar, 2019), which has good flexibility and tensile strength. It is beneficial to enhance specific surface and stability by loading CeO2 onto CC to form a 3D mesh electrode material.

In this work, the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh was successfully synthesized and applied in the specific detection of melamine. The photo-enhanced barrier effect on electrochemical response was explored, and a novel sensing method for melamine detection was developed. An outstanding sensing performance with the significantly enhanced sensitivity and accuracy was achieved. The CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh was promising for practical application as it exhibited good stability and high recovery rate in real detection of milk powder solution. It is a worthy approach to develop new highly sensitive sensors with strong resistance to interference in biological environments.

CC (1071HCB-Hydrophilic) was purchased from AvCard Materials Co., Ltd. (Massachusetts, USA). Melamine was purchased from Aladdin Holding Group Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Cerium(III) nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dopamine (DA) and Hemoglobin (Hb) were bought from Alfa Aesar Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Milk powder was purchased from supermarkets. Bought other regents in Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). To prevent experimental contamination, ultrapure water was used as all experimental water and all reagents are of analytical grade.

CC soaked overnight in nitric acid with a 10% mass score. The CC was washed in alcohol, anhydrous ethanol and deionized water and then placed in a 60°oven for 6 h. CeO2 was deposited in a three-electrode system, with 10 mM Ce(NO3)3·6H2O aqueous solution as electrolyte and CC as working electrode. The initial voltage of electrodeposition was −0.7 V.

1 g of citric acid solid was added into a 50 mL beaker. The beaker was placed in an oven and heated at 180 °C for 20 min. 50 mL of 0.5 mol/L NaOH solution was added to the beaker with stirring. 150 mL acetone was added to the prepared solution to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant was evaporated at 70 °C. The evaporated beaker was filled with 50 mL of deionized water. Finally, the prepared CNPs solution was refrigerated for use.

The electrolyte was obtained by adding 12 mL 30% H2O2 into 50 mL CNPs solution. The CNPs were electrodeposited in a three-electrode system where the CC/CeO2 mesh served as the working electrode and the deposition time was 1 h. The initial voltage of electrodeposition was −0.7 V.
The Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was adjusted to pH = 3.6 with 10% HCl. Finally, 0.25 g milk powder was added to 25 mL PBS.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out with a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). The detection of melamine was carried out in a three-electrode system with CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh as the working electrode, Pt as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. PBS with the concentration of 0.1 M and pH value as 3.6 was used as the electrolyte. All DPV tests were measured at a scan rate of 50 mV/s and the amplitude was 50 mV. The EIS was tested within the frequency of 0.01 Hz-100000 Hz and with an amplitude of 5 mV. The initial potential was −0.07 V.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were taken from a JSM-7601.Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using JEM-2100plus. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by the Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation). Infrared information was characterized by the Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained through the Thermo Scientific K-Alpha system. All electrochemical experiments were conducted at the electrochemical Workstation (CHI760E, Chenhua, Shanghai).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the prepared CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S1 and Scheme S1, the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh was fabricated by assembling layers of CC. CeO2 was electrochemically deposited on the surface of CC at 70 °C in a water bath and grown into CeO2 nanowires. CNPs were further deposited electrochemically on CeO2 nanowires to form the CeO2/CNPs Schottky junction.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, CC has a three-dimensional structure consisted by several layers of interwoven carbon fibers, providing rich layered anchoring positions for CeO2 and CNPs. In addition, Carbon fiber has good electrical conductivity and can be used as an electronic transport channel. Fig. 1b shows that CeO2 nanowires (diameter ∼ 200 nm, length ∼ 700 nm) are evenly distributed on the CC. The further deposited CNPs on CeO2 nanowires do not change the whole morphology (Fig. 1e) due to the small size and low content. As shown in Fig. 1d, the whole morphology of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh is similar to the CC substrate. The TEM image (Fig. 1c) further confirms the uniform distribution of CNPs on the surface of CeO2. The (200) facet of CeO2 can be identified by the 0.270 nm lattice fringes in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1f)(Dong, Zhu, Jia, Li, Ma, & Zhao, 2021). The HRTEM image of a single CNP in Fig. S2 shows that the lattice space is ∼ 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of CNPs (R. Wang, Lu, Zhang, Tang, & Xu, 2018). The EDS images show that C, Ce and O elements are uniformly distributed on the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh (Fig. S3).

As shown in Fig. S4a, the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh has distinct CeO2 peaks at 2θ = 28.6°, 33.1°, 47.5°, 56.3°, 59.1°, 69.4°, 77.0° and 79.2°, corresponding to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (420) planes of the fluorite cubic structure (JCPDS NO. 42–1002) (Bai, Wang, Han, Xie, & Diao, 2021). Two major peaks at 2θ = 26.2° and 44.4° are assigned to the (002) and (101) planes of C, respectively (Zhang et al., 2015). A small diffraction peak at 20° is from CNPs (Fig. S5) (Shang, Zhao, Qu, Li, & Chen, 2019), and the weak intensity is due to the relatively small CNPs content and low crystallinity.

Fig. S4b shows the FTIR spectrum of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh. CNPs contain many hydrophilic groups on their surface, so they have great hydrophilic properties (S. Zhao et al., 2015). The peak at 3226 cm−1 corresponds to the OH bond, indicating that CNPs successfully adsorb on the surface (Bai, Wang, Han, Xie, & Diao, 2021). Since the vibrational stretching of CC bond generated by sp2 hybrid carbon of CNPs corresponds to peaks at 1634 cm−1 and 1478 cm−1 (Bai, Wang, Han, Xie, & Diao, 2021). The peaks at 2964 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 correspond to the tensile vibration and bending vibration of CH bond, respectively (Dong, Zhu, Jia, Li, Ma, & Zhao, 2021). These absorption peaks indicate the successful deposition of CNPs onto the CC/CeO2 mesh surface. The spike at 661 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the Ce-O bond (S. Wang, et al., 2022) and demonstrates the successful load of CeO2 on the CC. Fig. S6 shows the FTIR spectrum of the CC/CNPs mesh. The broad band at 3226 cm−1 in Fig. S4b is much stronger than that in Fig. S6. The results show that electrodeposition of CeO2 causes more nanoparticles to be loaded on the CC.

XPS is used to characterize the chemical state of element. The full scan spectrum of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh shows the presence of C, O and Ce elements (Fig. S4c). As shown in Fig. S4d, the C 1s (Qian et al., 2016) spectrum shows two peaks at 284.8 and 286.0 eV, which are assigned to the CC sp2 hybridization of CNPs with CO bonds. The O 1s (Dong, Zhu, Jia, Li, Ma, & Zhao, 2021) peak at 528.48 eV and 530.78 eV represent the lattice oxygen (OL) and surface oxygen (OS), corresponding to the O2– bond and the CO bond (Fig. S4e). The Ce 3d (Li et al., 2019) spectrum (Fig. S4f) can be inversely folded into eight peaks. The four spectrum bands of U represent Ce 3d3/2, such as U'''(915.88 eV), U''(906.68 eV), U'(900.98 eV) and U (899.88 eV). V represents Ce 3d5/2, such as V'''(897.48 eV), V''(887.98 eV), V'(883.68 eV) and V (881.48 eV). In addition, the peaks label U''', U'', U, V''', V'' and V are assigned to Ce signals, while U' and V' are assigned to Ce3+ signals. The well-fitted peaks of Ce3+ and Ce4+ confirm the successful preparation of CeO2.

Electrostatic induction can change the energy band position of semiconductor by inducing charge, which can be produced by charged substance. Since Hb has an equipotential point (pl) of 7.4 (M. Zhao, et al., 2020), it is positively charged, uncharged, and negatively charged at pH = 5.8, 7.4, and 8.0. The regulatory effect of Schottky barrier on electrochemical response was studied in the electrochemical detection of dopamine (DA). DA is used as REDOX medium to ensure REDOX occurs (Ding et al., 2016). Different charged Hb is added to produce electrostatic induction to regulate barrier height. As shown in Fig. 2 a-c, it is found that positively charged Hb increases the oxidation peak current, but negatively charged Hb decreases the oxidation peak current. The uncharged Hb do not change the oxidation peak current. The results are analyzed by the energy band diagram (Fig. 2d-f). Schottky barrier (Φs) exists at the interface of CeO2/CNPs (Fig. 2e). Electrostatic induction of the positively charged Hb induces negative charge in the conduction band of CeO2 and causes it decrease. As a result, the barrier height decreases (ΔΦs < 0). This decreased Φs reduces the electrochemical impedance and boosts the electrochemical current (Fig. 2d). Accordingly, negatively charged Hb induces positive charges in CeO2, which causes the increased barrier height (ΔΦs > 0) and the decreased electrochemical current response (Fig. 2f). Uncharged Hb cannot produce electrostatic induction, so it cannot affect the electrochemical current by changing barrier height (Fig. 2e). The above results demonstrate that electrostatic induction can be used to adjust the electrochemical current response by regulating Φs.

The CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh was used to detect melamine in PBS at pH = 3.6, in which melamine was positively charged as its pKa = 5 (M. Zhao, et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. S7, the addition of 40 nM melamine causes the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) current decrease as 66.1 μA, and the addition of 80 nM melamine causes the DPV current decrease as 121 μA. The results demonstrate the addition of positively charged melamine can effectively produce the corresponding electrochemical current response. The CC/CeO2 mesh and the CC/CNPs (Fig. S8) mesh without Schottky barrier are further used to detect melamine, but little response is found. The results demonstrate it is the Schottky barrier that drives the corresponding electrochemical response.

When melamine is added, it will be adsorbed by CNPs as its triamine group can form carbon–nitrogen bonds with CNPs (Xu, Wei, Du, Li, Ji, & Hu, 2013) (Fig. 3 a). When positively charged melamine is adsorbed by CNPs, electrons are captured from CeO2 by electrostatic interaction. The loss of electrons causes the conduction band of CeO2 rise and the Schottky barrier height increase (ΔΦs > 0). As barrier blocks electron transport, the change of its height (ΔΦ) will affect the electrochemical current response by changing the electrochemical impedance. As a result, the increased Schottky barrier (ΔΦs > 0) increases the electrochemical impedance and decreases the electrochemical current response (Fig. 3b). It is further verified by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test. As shown in Fig. S9, the impedance value of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh increases as the concentration of positively charged melamine increases. In addition, we added the detection of neutral and negatively charged melamine, and the results were shown in Fig. S10. It is observed that negatively charged melamine increases the DPV current. The uncharged melamine does not change the DPV current. The results were explained by the following mechanism. The adsorption of negatively charged melamine would decrease Schottky barrier height (ΔΦs < 0) and drive the increased electrochemical current response. Uncharged melamine could not change the Schottky barrier height (ΔΦs = 0) by electrostatic induction, so there was little change in the electrochemical current response. The above discussions demonstrate melamine can be detected by using the electrostatic induced change of barrier height to drive electrochemical current response.

The influence of illumination on melamine detection was further explored. As shown in Fig. 4 a-c, the electrochemical response of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh to 40 nM positively charged melamine under a 200 W Xe lamp is greater than that in the dark, and it is further enhanced by 400 W Xe lamp irradiation. It was found that the change of electrochemical impedance was enhanced by illumination (Fig. 4d) as 2654 Ω in the dark and 3606 Ω under illumination. The increased change of electrochemical impedance caused the increased ΔΦs, which resulted in the enhanced electrochemical response. In addition, we adjusted the test frequency of EIS to the range of 0.001 Hz-100000 Hz, and the results were basically consistent with those before adjustment (Fig. S11). The above results demonstrate that illumination can enhance the electrochemical response to melamine through increasing ΔΦs (Fig. 4e).

The sensing mechanism is further discussed under illumination. Under illumination, lots of photogenerated electrons are excited to the conduction band of CeO2, and electrons aggregation in the conduction band leads to the reduced barrier height (ΔΦs < 0). The adsorption of melamine with the same concentration will capture more electrons from the conduction band of CeO2, causing the barrier height to increase even more (ΔΦs > 0) (Fig. 4f). The increased ΔΦs causes the enhanced electrochemical current response, so illumination improves the sensing performance.

The specificity test of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh is shown in Fig. S12a, it is found that the possible co-existing disruptors do not actually interfere with melamine detection. The results indicate that the proposed sensing mode has the strong resistance to interference in milk powder solution.

The strong resistance to interference can be attributed to the following points. The electrochemical response is excited by the barrier change caused by selective adsorption of melamine. Other charged interferors cannot be absorbed, so the barrier change cannot be excited to cause interference signals. Because it is a process in which the electrochemical response is excited by physical electrostatic induction, the electrochemically active substance cannot cause actual interference to the process. According to the Schottky barrier-current density formula J = AT2 exp(−qΦ/kT) [exp(qV/kT) − 1] (M. Zhao, et al., 2020), the change of barrier height (ΔΦ) excites an exponentially amplified electrochemical response. Illumination further improves sensitivity by increasing barrier regulation. This is equivalent to interference being suppressed.

As shown in Fig. S12b, the electrochemical current response is almost unchanged after 20 cycles, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 0.661%. In addition, CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh maintains 86.2% DPV response to melamine after 14 days (Fig. S12c). The above results show that the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh has the good short-term and long-term stability.

The actual detection of melamine is carried out in milk powder solution under 200 W Xe lamp irradiation. As shown in Table S1, the recoveries of the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh in milk powder solution range from 98.2% to 104% with RSD from 1.49% to 7.01%. In addition, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) is used to test the milk powder solution with 80 nM melamine, and the results are shown in Table S2. The recovery rate of GC–MS is 98.9% and RSD is 4.13%, which is consistent with the reported results (J. Li, Qi, & Shi, 2009) (Xia et al., 2009). The high recovery indicates the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh is reliable and has a promising application prospect.

The CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh with Schottky barrier was successfully fabricated for detection of melamine in milk powder solution. It was found that electrostatically induced change of barrier could drive the corresponding electrochemical current response, and illumination could enhance the barrier driving effect significantly. The novel electrochemical sensing mechanism based on the photo-enhanced barrier driving effect achieved the significantly improved sensing performance to melamine with strong resistance to interference. Sensitivity was increased as 4.98 times and LOD was decreased by 73.3%. The obtained high recovery rate (100%-104%) and good stability indicated the CC/CeO2/CNPs mesh was promising for practical application. It provides a novel sensing mode for developing sensors with resistance to interference by using the photo-enhanced barrier driving effect on electrochemical response.

Yichang He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Yiquan Liao: Visualization, Validation. Bin Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Ruiqi Xu: Supervision. Ye Ma: Investigation, Supervision. Minggang Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Hongzhi Cui: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52072353, 22106152); Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (202042009, 202113030); Fellowship of China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M672146); Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2021QB059).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137246.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
